comment.body:
So because financial markets don’t operate in a normal distribution, you have to account for that discrepancy using something other than a standard geometric brownian motion model (Gaussian distribution). t-distribution is much more similar to modeling markets because the tail ends of the curve (outliers in price movements) are typically higher. Not only that but volatility tends to cluster, and so you have to account for that fact as well (hence the HMM model). 

The HMM I’m building is integrated with my  GARCH model and OU process, but the problem I’m having is the python for HMM’s is simple and can only use Gaussian distribution, but I can create a mixed model with fatter tails to be a better model for financial markets. 

Pomegranate library from MIT is much more flexible and I could directly code in t-distribution but for some reason I cannot seem to import and run field from anaconda through python (I think anaconda was destined for Linux and that’s why). Just trying to see the best approach to this problem

Not only that but volatility tends to cluster, and so you have to account for that fact as well (hence the HMM model). 

The HMM I’m building is integrated with my  GARCH model and OU process, but the problem I’m having is the python for HMM’s is simple and can only use Gaussian distribution, but I can create a mixed model with fatter tails to be a better model for financial markets.

comment.body: If vol of vol is high and market makers are net short gamma (negative GEX) due to inverse vanna flows heading into July MOpEx, wouldn't they need to buy VX futures to hedge, which should theoretically increase volatility? But your post suggests that the current dynamics are supportive of the market, with vol spikes capped and dealers getting shorter delta if $SPX holds above certain levels - how does that all fit together?

comment.body:
My understanding:
When vol of vol is elevated, the short vanna effect tends to be significant—especially for calls above spot. This implies that dealers are holding a large number of long VIX futures, beta-hedged with short ES positions.

Assuming vol of vol begins to mean-revert, the unwinding of these hedges would involve selling VIX futures and buying ES, which creates supportive flow for the market.
